summary: All round-1 findings resolved. R1-01 and R1-04 fixed correctly. R1-03 defense is technically correct — the reviewer's original finding was wrong. The test is sound. Suite is approved.

R1-01.assessment: vm.assume threshold correctly changed from > 0xff to > 9. Comment rewritten to name precompiles [1..9] specifically. Accepted.

R1-02.assessment: Confirmed correct — expectEmit masks intentionally differ between events with and without indexed parameters.

R1-03.assessment: The contract-tester's empirical defense is correct and the reviewer's original R1-03 finding was technically wrong. The full call chain is: vault.withdraw() [ENTERED] → safeTransfer(dev, net) → safeTransfer(malicious, fee) → ERC20._update → onUsdcReceived() → reentryCount++ → target.withdraw() [REVERTS: ReentrancyGuardReentrantCall]. The revert from target.withdraw() propagates through onUsdcReceived, through _update, through safeTransfer, and unwinds the entire outer vault.withdraw() call. Cross-contract storage writes (reentryCount++ on MockMaliciousTreasury) that occur within the reverting call tree ARE rolled back — there is no call boundary that would preserve MockMaliciousTreasury's storage across the revert. The EVM's state journal is per-transaction, not per-contract-instance. The empirical confirmation (`0 != 1` failure on assertEq) is conclusive. The updated comment is precise and correctly explains the selector-as-proof argument. No code change required — the test was correct in round 1.

R1-04.assessment: Clarifying comment added to VaultHandler.withdrawFromVault explaining gross-vs-net accounting and why gross is the right unit for the integrity invariant. Accepted.

overall_assessment: Suite is approved. 51 tests passing. 100% branch coverage on both contracts. All spec requirements met. The reentrancy test correctly relies on the unique ReentrancyGuardReentrantCall selector as proof of hook-and-block — no alternative path in PaymentVaultImpl can produce that selector. Ready for security-auditor review.
